title: The $10 Trillion Job Every Mother Does for Free (And Why It's Time to Name It)
guestBio: Dr. Danielle Dowling, Psy.D. is a Los Angeles-based certified life coach and doctor of psychology with over 14 years and 15,000+ hours of coaching experience. She helps high-achieving women and mothers reconnect with their self-worth and build lives they genuinely feel proud of. She is the author of the upcoming book Good Girl, Bad Mom.
excerpt: The invisible workload of motherhood is worth over $10 trillion annually, yet society conditions women to see overwhelm as personal failure. Dr. Danielle Dowling reveals why naming this labor isn't betrayal—it's dignity.
metaDescription: Discover why the invisible workload of motherhood is worth $10 trillion and how naming this unpaid labor transforms overwhelm into empowerment.
keyTakeaways: Naming motherhood as work is not a betrayal of love—it's an act of dignity and self-worth.
Women globally perform 12.5 billion hours of unpaid labor daily, valued at over $10 trillion annually.
Self-abandonment disguised as virtue is still self-abandonment and erases women's identities.
Replace asking for 'help' with asking partners to 'own' responsibilities to transfer real accountability.
The progression of self-worth to self-respect to self-care creates sustainable boundaries and wellness.
Good girl conditioning transforms systemic overwhelm into personal shame instead of warranted critique.
Raising independent children requires mothers to stop doing everything for them and teach responsibility.

pullQuotes.text: Naming motherhood as work is not a betrayal of love—it's an act of dignity.
You don't have a need problem. You have a conditioned deprioritization problem.
Self-abandonment dressed as virtue is still self-abandonment.

timestamps.label: Why Loving Your Child Doesn't Mean You Can't Name the Labor
Motherhood's Org Chart: The Six-Figure Jobs You're Doing for Free
Good Girl Conditioning: Why Overwhelm Becomes Personal Shame
The Fourth Trimester No One Talks About
Self-Worth → Self-Respect → Self-Care: The Right Order

content:
What if I told you that the exhaustion you feel as a mother isn't a personal failing, but evidence of a $10 trillion job you're doing without pay, recognition, or even acknowledgment? This revolutionary perspective forms the heart of a powerful conversation between Aimee Rickabus and Dr. Danielle Dowling on The Manage Her podcast—one that every overwhelmed mother needs to hear.

Dr. Dowling, a doctor of psychology with over 15,000 hours of coaching high-achieving women, brings something motherhood has never received: a proper job description. When you break down the invisible workload mothers carry daily, you're looking at roles that would command six-figure salaries in any corporate setting. HR manager at $140,000 annually. Project manager at $100,000. Management analyst at $102,000. Yet when mothers perform these exact functions for their families, society wraps it in the language of love and tells them it shouldn't feel like work.

The Trillion-Dollar Reality of Unpaid Labor

The numbers are staggering and validating in equal measure. According to Oxfam data that Dr. Dowling references, women and girls globally perform 12.5 billion hours of unpaid labor daily. Daily. When economists assign monetary value to this work, it totals over $10 trillion annually. This isn't abstract theorizing—this is the concrete worth of what mothers do every single day without compensation, appreciation, or even basic recognition.

Yet culture has conditioned us to see naming this labor as somehow unseemly. Dr. Dowling challenges this conditioning head-on: acknowledging the work doesn't diminish the love. In fact, it honors both the love and the person doing the loving. When we refuse to name motherhood as labor, we participate in our own erasure.

Why Good Girl Conditioning Keeps Us Silent

The reason so many mothers internalize overwhelm as personal failure rather than systemic injustice comes down to what Dr. Dowling calls "good girl conditioning." From childhood, women learn that their worth comes from serving others' needs while minimizing their own. This conditioning runs so deep that when mothers feel overwhelmed by impossible workloads, they blame themselves rather than questioning the system.

This self-abandonment gets rebranded as virtue. The woman who disappears inside everyone else's needs is praised as selfless, while the woman who maintains boundaries is labeled selfish. Dr. Dowling exposes this false binary for what it is: a way to keep women compliant and invisible. True service to your family includes serving yourself—not from selfishness, but from the recognition that you matter too.

The Language That Changes Everything

One of the most practical shifts Dr. Dowling offers involves changing how we communicate about household responsibilities. Instead of asking your partner "Can you help me with dinner?" try "Do you mind owning dinner tonight?" This isn't semantic game-playing—it's the difference between positioning yourself as the default responsible party who occasionally gets assistance, versus transferring actual ownership of the task.

When you ask for help, you remain the project manager, the one who carries mental load even when someone else performs the physical task. When you ask someone to own something, you transfer both the execution and the cognitive burden. This simple language swap can begin redistributing the invisible workload that exhausts so many mothers.

The Right Order: Self-Worth, Self-Respect, Then Self-Care

Perhaps the most transformative framework Dr. Dowling shares involves reordering how we approach self-care. Most advice jumps straight to bubble baths and spa days, but sustainable change requires building from the foundation up. Self-worth is the belief that you matter. Self-respect is the behavior that proves it. Self-care is the sustainable lifestyle of someone who has established the first two.

This progression explains why surface-level self-care doesn't stick for most mothers. You can't maintain boundaries and wellness practices if you fundamentally don't believe you deserve them. The work begins with recognizing your inherent worth, then consistently acting in ways that honor that worth, which naturally leads to sustainable self-care practices.

Redefining the "Bad Mom"

Dr. Dowling's upcoming book title, "Good Girl, Bad Mom," captures a profound truth: the behaviors that make us "good girls"—endless accommodation, self-sacrifice, people-pleasing—often make us exhausted, resentful mothers. The "bad mom" in this context isn't actually bad at all. She's simply a healthy, boundaried human who refuses to disappear inside everyone else's needs.

This reframe is quietly revolutionary. It suggests that perhaps the problem isn't mothers who feel overwhelmed, but a system that demands they perform impossible amounts of unpaid labor while smiling gratefully for the privilege. When mothers start setting boundaries and prioritizing their own needs alongside their families', they're not failing at motherhood—they're modeling healthy humanity.

The Training Ground Perspective

One of the most liberating insights Dr. Dowling offers involves reframing your home as a training ground for adulthood rather than a service hotel. When you do everything for your children, you're not loving them—you're borrowing time. Eventually, they need to function as independent adults, and every task you do for them that they could reasonably handle themselves is a missed opportunity to build that capacity.

This perspective transforms what feels like maternal guilt into purposeful preparation. Teaching your children to manage their own responsibilities isn't mean—it's essential. And it has the added benefit of reducing your own workload while building their competence and confidence.

Permission to Name What Is

If you've ever felt guilty for being overwhelmed by motherhood, this conversation offers something precious: permission to name the reality of your experience without shame. Your exhaustion isn't evidence of inadequacy—it's evidence of enormous, uncompensated labor. Your desire for recognition isn't selfishness—it's human dignity.

Dr. Dowling reminds us that naming the work is the first act of self-worth. When mothers stop minimizing what they do and start articulating its true scope and value, they begin reclaiming their own worth in the process. This isn't about loving your children less—it's about loving yourself enough to matter too.

The conversation between Aimee and Dr. Dowling offers both validation and actionable frameworks for change. It's permission to stop internalizing systemic problems as personal failures, and invitation to build a life where your worth isn't contingent on your willingness to disappear. For every mother who has ever felt overwhelmed and ashamed of it, this episode provides the language and frameworks to finally name what you've been carrying all along.

guestLinks.label: 📚 ORDER NOW (Released March 2, 2026) Good Girl, Bad Mom

quiz.title: What's Your Motherhood Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you navigate the invisible work of motherhood and what it reveals about your natural leadership approach. This quiz will help you recognize your strengths and understand why you deserve recognition for the trillion-dollar job you're already doing.

questions.question: When someone compliments you on how well you manage everything at home, your first internal reaction is:

options.text: A mix of pride and exhaustion — you appreciate the recognition but wonder why it feels so rare
Frustration that they're noticing your work but not stepping up to share the load
Gratitude, but also a mental note about what systems you could put in place so it's not all on you
Appreciation, while also making sure they see you as more than just someone who manages a household

questions.question: Your child asks you to do something they're perfectly capable of doing themselves. You:

options.text: Do it while mentally noting that you should probably stop doing this so much
Firmly redirect them to do it themselves and explain why that's important
Use it as a teaching moment about responsibility and help them create a system
Encourage their independence while staying connected to who they are as a person

questions.question: When you think about all the invisible work you do daily (meal planning, scheduling, emotional labor), you feel:

options.text: Conflicted — you love your family but sometimes wish someone would notice all you do
Clear that this work has real value and deserves recognition, not just gratitude
Motivated to create systems that distribute this work more fairly among family members
Proud of your skills while also protective of your identity outside of these tasks

questions.question: Your approach to asking your partner for help with household responsibilities tends to be:

options.text: You ask nicely but often end up doing it yourself to avoid conflict
You've stopped asking for 'help' and started assigning ownership of specific areas
You focus on creating shared systems where everyone knows their role without constant reminders
You communicate your needs clearly while maintaining your own interests and priorities

questions.question: When you take time for yourself, the voice in your head usually says:

options.text: You deserve this, but you also feel a little guilty and hope nothing important needs you
This is non-negotiable self-care and anyone who has a problem with it can figure it out
This recharge time makes you a better parent and partner, so it benefits everyone
This is essential for staying connected to who you are beyond your roles in the family

questions.question: If you had to describe your biggest growth area as a mother and leader, it would be:

options.text: Learning to name and value your contributions without feeling selfish
Continuing to hold firm boundaries even when others resist the changes
Building sustainable systems that don't rely on your constant management
Balancing deep love for your family with staying true to your individual identity

results.title: The Quiet Revolutionary
results.description: You're beginning to question why you feel guilty for wanting recognition for all you do. You sense something isn't quite right about carrying the mental load alone, but you're still learning to name it without shame. Your awareness is growing, and you're ready to challenge the 'good girl' conditioning that keeps you silent. This episode will give you the language and validation you need to start honoring your work as the valuable labor it truly is.

results.title: The Fierce Advocate
results.description: You've moved beyond asking for help to demanding ownership and accountability from others. You recognize your worth and aren't afraid to set boundaries, even when others push back. Your journey from self-worth to self-respect is well underway, and you're modeling powerful leadership for those around you. This episode will reinforce your approach and give you even more tools to articulate why your stand matters for all mothers.

results.title: The Conscious Builder
results.description: You understand that raising independent children means stepping back from doing everything for them, even when it's harder in the short term. You're actively working to break cycles and build systems that honor everyone's contributions. Your leadership style focuses on sustainable growth rather than quick fixes. This episode will validate your long-term thinking and help you see how your approach is reshaping family dynamics for the better.

results.title: The Identity Guardian
results.description: You've learned that self-abandonment disguised as virtue is still self-abandonment, and you refuse to erase yourself in the name of motherhood. You prioritize maintaining your identity alongside your role as a mother, understanding that both can coexist beautifully. Your leadership comes from wholeness rather than depletion. This episode will celebrate your commitment to staying true to yourself and show you how this choice benefits everyone in your family.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Today's conversation is what I've been wanting to have for a long time. Motherhood is a joy and it's also a job, and naming it as work doesn't strip it of its magic. It actually protects the magic by restoring dignity in the person doing it. Welcome back to The Manager podcast, the show where we turn invisible labor into visible leadership and women reclaim their time, worth, and power. I'm your host, Aimee Rickabus.

Today I'm joined by Dr. Danielle Dowling, a therapy-trained life coach, boy mom, and author of Good Girl, Bad Mom. Danielle has nearly 12,000 hours of coaching experience helping women rewrite old stories, build bold boundaries, and stop disappearing in the needs of everyone else. As I was reading her work alongside my own writing for The Manager, I kept thinking, we're speaking the same language - the language of naming what's real, de-shaming the labor of motherhood, and helping women reclaim self-worth and self-respect inside the lives they deeply love.

This practical episode is validating and quietly revolutionary, especially if you've ever loved your children fiercely and still felt overwhelmed, resentful, or invisible. Let's get into it. Welcome, Danielle. Thank you so much for being on the show.

**Danielle:** Thank you so much. I'm so honored to be here. I'm so happy.

**Aimee:** So Danielle, you talk so beautifully about how calling motherhood a job doesn't cancel the joy. Why do you think we've been taught that loving our children means we're not allowed to name the labor?

**Danielle:** This is a great question and one that I think is subconscious for so many women for sure, but I think in culture in general. We've been taught that if you really love your child, then you should love motherhood and you should do it with a smile. And that's what I call good girl conditioning - be grateful, be impressive, be agreeable, be low maintenance at all times. 

Then we're handed a role that requires everything of us all the time. When we inevitably feel overwhelmed, we internalize that as personal failing. So we don't say, "Hey, this is a massive workload." What she says, what she thinks is, "Something must be wrong with me. Maybe I'm bad at this." And then culture largely ignores the cost because it is built on the assumption that women will endure, because historically we have endured.

I like to think of it as culture treats motherhood as this sacred hobby rather than labor. So culture treats motherhood as sacred pastime rather than labor, and naming it as labor then for a woman can feel like betrayal. But it's not, because loving your child and then naming the work are two separate truths. Does that resonate?

**Aimee:** Yes, that resonates. Heck yeah. Preach mama. I love it. When we look at motherhood operationally, not sentimentally, what are the roles women are actually performing every day that would be recognized and compensated in any corporate setting?

**Danielle:** When you step back from it and really look at the work that you perform every day, the skills that it is comprised of, motherhood is essentially an entire corporate organization chart, except the pay is zero and the performance review is basically your own guilt.

In Good Girl, Bad Mom, chapter 10 is titled "Motherhood is a Joy, But It's Also a Job." I give motherhood something that it's never gotten before, which is a job description. I'm hoping that the listeners and women will take this to heart and really acknowledge the labor that goes into it.

There's so many different departments. You have operations - keeping the household running, managing systems, managing feelings, managing time. You are doing project management work - planning, prioritizing, coordinating schedules. I create a ton of that! When I really got into the weeds, I'm thinking, this takes up so much time. And then if something gets canceled, it's a house of cards, because then now you're reorganizing the entire calendar again.

You're anticipating needs as part of that project management role. You're doing logistics and supply chain - the toilet paper's missing, I got to go get it from the garage, which is where we store things, and bring it to all the bathrooms. Food, groceries, clothing rotations, appointments, school stuff, transportation.

You're HR. You're doing people management, emotional regulation, culture setting - you're setting the culture in your home. You're doing behavior shaping, training and development. Then you're also doing crisis management. Because who's getting up with the child at 2 a.m. in the morning when they have a fever? It's probably mom. Not always, but probably. Or whoever is in the role of mom, even if that is a man.

**Aimee:** Yeah, the default parent.

**Danielle:** Yes. So you're handling meltdowns, emergencies, and this constant readiness to pivot. I was never really good at this, and motherhood has taught me - really sort of dragged me by my hair. I went kicking and screaming to be much better at pivoting because I'm Type A. I bought my calendar. Everything's organized. Don't disrupt my calendar. So I just crashed and burned inside that lesson, but I've gotten much better.

You're doing executive level decision making all day long. You're making hundreds of micro decisions that no one sees because they don't really live on a calendar, but they live in your head. I'm on a mission to communicate to women, to moms specifically, but women also because women in general just take on too much, that these are not soft skills. Everything I just listed - and that's why I listed it in the book, I was like, just read this. This is 15 pages of what you do over the course of 25 years of your child's life. And that's just the first 25 years. You hope that they're more independent after that, but once a mom, always a mom.

These are not soft skills. These are paid roles with titles and salaries in the workplace. But at home, the default conditioning is that it is treated as a personality trait, or it's just called help. Recognizing and acknowledging that motherhood is not just magic, that it is a project, is essential in a woman's and in a mother's overall well-being.

**Aimee:** You and I were talking about the actual monetary value of all of these jobs too. Do you have those numbers?

**Danielle:** So if we were going to get paid to do the jobs that we're doing for free at home, that we're not even allowed to really say we're doing because of cultural conditioning, if we had to put a dollar sign on it - I looked this up because I don't have this memorized, but I'll read a few of them because it really is truly mind blowing.

If we were to look at a human resource manager - so this is people management, policy, hiring and firing, conflict mediation - this is the BLS, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reporting, a highly credible source from May 2024. A human resource manager gets paid $140,000 a year. That's the median salary.

A project management specialist - so those timelines, the calendar I was talking about earlier, prioritization, project planning, cross-functional coordination, shout out to the moms who have more than one child, talk about cross-functional - the median salary according to the BLS, $100,750 a year. These are six figures. There isn't one number on here that's lower than six figures.

Management analysis - so those SOPs that you talk about in The Manager, what is the system I am following every morning to wake my child up, get them ready for school and get them to school on time? That's a system. What needs to be done between 6 a.m. and 8 a.m. drop off in order for that to be a successful morning. You need a system for bath time, dinner time, homework, sports. This counts. 

So constant decision making, resource allocation - this has the median salary of $102,950 a year. I'll send all of these - there's seven on here. Six figures.

**Aimee:** Six figure salaries.

**Danielle:** Six figure salaries. I also took a look at invisible labor a little bit closer. I looked at federal time use data - the American Federal Time Use Data Survey that surveys how Americans spend their time. There is data that shows us that women spend on average between five and eight hours performing unpaid household and caregiving labor a day.

On the conservative end, that's 35 hours a week of unpaid labor, invisible labor. If we were going to value that at the median wage of $25 an hour, which it's not by the way - we're talking about six-figure salaries here - but if we were just looking at the 35 hours a week times $25 an hour, we're looking at a value of $45,000 a year, which is much lower than what we just reported. But it just goes to show you that on the extremely conservative end, women are performing $45,000 a year of unpaid, invisible labor.

If we were actually going to assign a title to the skills that she performs inside motherhood, then we can start extrapolating and saying, okay, a human resource manager in the workplace is paid $140,000 a year. These are really important conversations for women to have. And it is difficult for them to name it as work. I think it's hard for them to admit that this is hard.

**Aimee:** Why don't we name it as work? And why does admitting this is hard feel so scary or shameful for us?

**Danielle:** For a couple of reasons. Culture has motherhood wrapped up in this fantasy that motherhood should be natural, that it should be effortless and that it should be spiritually fulfilling at all times. So when we ask a woman, when we ask a mother to name it as labor, as work, it can feel like we're sort of ruining something sacred.

Then we layer on top of that what I call good girl conditioning, which all women, most women receive, spoken or unspoken, that it is our job to be agreeable and impressive and low maintenance at all times. So then if you're struggling, you must not be grateful enough. If you need help, you're failing. If you complain, you're most definitely difficult. This is how women internalize it.

Even if nobody outside externally is saying that out loud, they're probably thinking that because they too have drank the medieval Kool-Aid and have a general perspective that motherhood should be natural for a woman, should be effortless for a woman. She should be happy being a mother.

But the truth is, the work stays invisible because that's how the system stays comfortable. If we were to collectively name the workload, we would have to collectively respond to it. And this is where it gets tricky. We would have to respond to it at home, we would have to respond to it at work and in policy, which would be very inconvenient for the system, since the system, the world sort of runs on women and girls' unpaid labor.

Oxfam has this interesting data that reported that women and girls do an estimated 12.5 billion hours of unpaid labor a day. This is globally, which is valued at over $10 trillion a year annually. If you stop doing your 35 hours a week, someone's going to notice. If all women and girls stop doing just that 35, planes would fall from the sky. The world would stop spinning on its axis, but culture just largely ignores it.

So women end up swallowing the truth of the labor and they just sort of smile. We have a hard time calling it work because there's this collective cultural fantasy about motherhood, what it should mean and be for a woman. And then we've got that good girl conditioning which just says, if I speak up, I'm difficult. If I'm struggling, then I'm failing. It's not the system. It's me. So I make it a personal failing. I point the overwhelm and the anxiety inwards. I go, it's me. I'm not good enough. Hence good girl conditioning.

You get resentful of the work too, because you don't even feel like you can communicate you're overwhelmed because if you do, then you're not a good girl, you're not being impressive, you're not being agreeable. You're probably making somebody uncomfortable. You might disappoint someone.

**Aimee:** And then planes fall from the sky. We should really think about this more. We really need to challenge the worst case scenario.

**Danielle:** Totally. I think there's a version of this - and I say this in the book too - that if I acquiesce to my shame and my guilt and my resentment, and I have a need and articulate it, or I'm angry and I don't exactly swallow it, or I say no or set a limitation or set a boundary, I'm going to be a lonely cat lady living on the cold hard streets of Orange County. The brain kind of goes there. It's a nervous system response.

**Aimee:** You will be left behind. Your tribe will leave you. You have no value.

**Danielle:** Absolutely. There's part of that. And it's probably some really ancient DNA that we're carrying with us from 10,000 years ago. I think it goes back to the days where we roamed the earth in tribes of 17 people. If you were excommunicated from the tribe, you were definitely going to die in the Serengeti somewhere.

**Aimee:** In The Manager, I write, "I often know what I want, but I'm not valuing my needs at the same level of importance as other people." When you hear that through the lens of your work with women and mothers, what do you think is happening psychologically for me in that moment?

**Danielle:** What I hear - I don't think a woman is confused about her needs. You give any woman or any person a quiet room, non-interrupted, with maybe a little gentle music and a couple of deep breaths, and she's going to be able to articulate what she needs pretty quickly. So I don't think it's that we don't know our needs. I think that she's conditioned to deprioritize them. I think women are conditioned to deprioritize our needs. And I think mothers most certainly are conditioned to deprioritize their needs.

Psychologically, it's often a mix of that good girl training and that nervous system safety. It just doesn't feel safe. A lot of women, we've learned early that belonging comes from being easy to be around. It comes from being helpful and being pleasing and being pleasant and being low maintenance. 

So even when she knows what she wants, give her that quiet room uninterrupted for nine minutes, to value what she needs at the same level as everyone else around her just feels unsafe. When you lean into those conversations with her, she understands logically it doesn't feel unsafe. She gets that. She's smart. It feels relationally unsafe. It's that nervous system.

Because if I take up space, then I might disappoint someone, and then I become a lonely cat lady living on the street with a tin can. It's a really quick spiral there. If I say no, I'm going to make somebody mad and then I immediately become unvaluable and my partner's going to leave me. It's just a quick - nervous system just jumps real quick there. If I ask for more, I'm definitely too much, and again, I'm going to die without family and friends.

Her body, my body, probably your body, most women's bodies, we choose this old strategy - back to what you said a few moments ago, it's this ancient conditioning. We choose this old strategy of abandoning herself to keep the peace, which I have done hand over fist, especially in the early days of motherhood.

**Aimee:** Me too.

**Danielle:** Then we call that abandoning myself to keep the peace, and then we label that being a good mom. Self-abandonment is good mom-ness. It really is. It's like the more you are a martyr, the more you sacrifice yourself and don't do self-care and just do for everyone else. That's how you become a good mom.

**Aimee:** Good mom, good partner, good woman.

**Danielle:** Good woman, yes. Good girl conditioning knows no bounds. It doesn't stop just at motherhood. We're really trained long before we're in girlhood. We're trained long before we become mothers. The good mom in that context is just a good girl with a diaper bag. Always agreeable, always pleasing and selfless to the point of self-erasure.

That's exactly what it is. It's self-erasure kind of dressed up as virtue. And we need to start saying that out loud so we can advocate from a place of reality versus a place of shame, because she doesn't speak up because she internalizes that anxiety and that overwhelm and that struggle as a personal failing. That's very embarrassing to her. So her answer to that is just to try harder.

**Aimee:** So we're basically internalizing our overwhelm. We're saying, "That's my personal failure," instead of recognizing that the workload that's been put on us and the cultural expectations that surround motherhood are unrealistic.

**Danielle:** That's right. It's unrealistic. And instead we're saying I'm failing because of this. The thing that I really realized, my breakthrough, my aha moment with myself when I was writing The Manager was maybe if I ask for help, he won't leave. And then he didn't. Maybe if I ask my kids to help me more, they'll still love me. Maybe they can take the trash out more, make their beds, pick up their dishes, wash their dishes, be self-motivated with their homework. Maybe they can, if I expect them to do those things, maybe they can. And maybe I won't disappoint them. Maybe I won't be a failure to them.

That was my aha in the middle of writing that book - wow, this old story that's buried deep somewhere in my mitochondrial DNA that tells me that the whole tribe is going to leave me, they're going to put me on a piece of ice and they're going to shove me into the ocean and I'm going to float away to my doom. Maybe this isn't the truth. Maybe this is some old fear that doesn't belong here and we need to shine a big fat spotlight on this thing and get rid of it. It would be so liberating. It would be so freeing.

In this context, the bad mom sort of flies in the face of all that good girl conditioning, because she breaks every rule. She has needs and she admits them out loud. She gets angry and she doesn't always swallow it. She says no, she sets limits. She sometimes disappoints people. She doesn't perform perfection on command. And in reality, and I think this is what you're saying, in reality, she's just a fully formed human who is honest and boundaried, but still deeply loving and available to her family. She's just not - in many ways, she's the healthiest version of me.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it's important we make room for that. I've realized so much of my life was performative. I didn't even know that word before. And I'm thinking, wow, I'm performing like I'm a Disney princess working in the show at Disneyland. Maybe I'm allowed to be authentically me in my house. Maybe my children and my husband need me to show up as my authentic self rather than the performance version.

**Danielle:** Yes, I have certainly performed for worth the majority of my life. I would say up until three years ago when I just started cracking into the themes in my book and the themes in your book. I'm almost 50 this year, so I mean, that was at 46 and a half. It's the season of our life. I wonder as our estrogen level began to drop that we were able to see something through that veil, that veil lifted possibly. Because mine hit me at 45. I was like, what the heck am I doing?

I think motherhood is the Super Bowl of good girl conditioning. I think that by around 45, you're just fucking exhausted. So I think yes to the estrogen. I think it's probably a very wise culmination of a few things. Like 45-ish, you're like, "What the fuck? I can't keep going on like this."

Because you can't performance manage a role that has no finish line. The work is relentless, it's constant. And so the good girl will literally, which I've tried doing, I'm sure you have too, I'm sure your listeners have as well, will literally burn herself to the ground to try to be good at something that quite honestly was never meant to be carried alone.

If we refer back to what you said earlier about just ancient conditioning or even my little joke about roaming the earth in tribes of 17 people. I mean, women were much more supported in motherhood in ancient times. It really was a collective endeavor, but in modern history, we're sort of on our own little islands in these individual houses, suffering under the weight of good girl conditioning. Therefore we feel like we have to bite our tongue around how difficult it is, but we don't have the support of our sisters that we used to.

**Aimee:** I do think it takes a village. Our moms and our aunties and all the people we used to have. Our elders. Our grandmothers.

**Danielle:** It's really so kind of sad to me when I think about it sometimes. I really feel the loss of the possibility that we should have had, what would have been possible for us as people in this moment in time that we could have had. Our parents could have had bigger families. We could have had more siblings. We could have created a culture that was intentional, that understood the need of the human species as a social animal, that this social structure actually works better for us than the nuclear family, which is a modern concept that I think fails for fathers and mothers. You see the divorce rates. It doesn't seem like it's working.

**Aimee:** I agree.

**Danielle:** In Good Girl, Bad Mom, you make a powerful distinction. Self-care isn't the first step. Self-worth and self-respect are. Can you walk us through that framework and why starting there changes everything?

I think once you learn the value of labor, it becomes kind of obvious why self-care tips don't really stick. Because when you don't feel worthy of care, because if I'm a good girl and if I'm struggling, if I'm feeling like I need to ask for help, if I'm feeling overwhelmed, if I'm feeling anxious, then it's probably my fault that I'm feeling that way. Something's wrong with me. So I'm not really worthy. I haven't lived up to the task. I'm not being a good mom. So I don't really feel like I can stop. I can rest. I can reward myself. I just need to try harder.

When you don't feel worthy of care, you treat that care like a reward that you earn after everything is done. But as I just said, you can't performance manage a role that has no finish line. Mom is never done. And so you're never resting.

In my framework, self-worth equates to the belief that I matter. And then self-respect is behaviors that prove I matter. And then finally, the last integration is self-care. Self-care becomes the lifestyle, not the first step, but it becomes the lifestyle of someone that has aligned the previous two.

First I see myself as someone inherently worthy and deserving of care. And then I behave like I matter. This could be just practical, real world. It could be three to nine minute self-care rituals. It doesn't have to be anything big that demonstrate I matter. And you do that consistently over time. And now you've created a self-caring lifestyle.

So naming motherhood legitimate work is a self-worth move. It's a woman saying, "This is real. I'm real. I count. This matters." And then the self-respect follows. The boundaries, the language, the choices that protect me, that protect her inside the reality of the job.

This is why so many people quote unquote fail at self-care because they're putting the cart before the horse. Self-care is just not sustainable unless I truly believe or am doing my darndest to practice the belief that I am worthy of being cared for. This is why people fall off. And then culture doesn't really have an answer to why self-care doesn't stick except, again, try harder.

**Aimee:** Totally. If I - what do I need today that I'm pretending that I don't?

**Danielle:** Yes, absolutely. I explored this concept in The Manager in that even naming motherhood as a legitimate act of work is also an act of self-worth. Just saying that it's work, which you're doing and I'm doing, is saying, hey, that's self-worth. And how does simply saying "this counts" begin to restore a woman's sense of value?

It's you refusing to minimize your own life. Naming the job as an act of self-worth is because naming it is dignity. It's dignity. And it's a woman saying, again, "This counts and I count."

Culturally, we were kind of gaslighting women by making them say that motherhood wasn't a job, because if a man was doing it, I think he would say, "Whoa, this is work, for free." Again, we women and girls do - let's look at the Oxfam stat again - we do 12.5 billion hours of unpaid invisible labor a day. If women and girls started asking for some recognition, some reward and some rest, the fear is that the world will stop spinning. Listen, it's going to require money. It's going to require reorg. It would be work, but it'd be work well worth it.

**Aimee:** It's important work. If we're going to really move forward and have this equal future for men and women that has been promised, then this is one of the things that needs a course correction. This is one of the leftover bits of the patriarchy that no longer serves us as humanity. It's not working for men and it's not working for women because for the men, this idea that we don't need help and that with motherhood the mother fairy came over and gave us magical powers on the day our baby was born - now you're magic and you don't need sleep or shower, you don't need food, you don't need to pee or poop, you can do it all, you've got this girl 24/7 365 and you should love it and you should be really good at it from the beginning. Really good. Love every minute of it, cherish everything. It's so beautiful.

It's so hard to feel, especially for - I had postpartum anxiety and depression in the beginning. And I just was out of my mind with guilt and shame about it, because I had drank the Kool-Aid and I'm a part of culture and I too was wrapped up in the fantasy, the cultural fantasy that motherhood should be natural and effortless and fulfilling at all times. And I am a prime example. I internalized it as me not feeling that way, not loving it, not feeling like I was a natural at it. I internalized that as a personal failing. I felt like I'm not good at this.

**Danielle:** Me too. I remember thinking the first 30 days of being a mom, "Why would anybody ever want to do this? Why did I think this was a good idea? This is really hard."

**Aimee:** It's really hard. I remember thinking while I was giving birth, I can't believe people do this again.

**Danielle:** Birth was easy for me. For me, it was everything afterwards. It was the healing part, actually, of afterbirth. I had a really hard healing period after my first baby. I had 30 stitches. It was not fun. No one - I was not prepared for the nipple soreness. I was not prepared for the undercarriage situation. I was so ready for birth. I was really prepared for pregnancy. I was really prepared for birth and I was really unprepared for the fourth trimester.

**Aimee:** Yeah, woefully unprepared.

**Danielle:** And I talk about that in the book too. I say, "Was I supposed to know?" Which also made me feel shameful and like a bad mom. And I'm thinking, maybe I didn't do enough research. Maybe I didn't talk to enough people. Why am I feeling blindsided by my reality right now?

I had to have an emergency C-section. I couldn't stand up straight for almost three weeks. And I write about this in the book. I said, by the way, C-section is considered major surgery. I mean, they take all your organs out. They're on your chest. Then they got to put them all back. Hopefully they put it back right. It went well for me, but when you think about it, you're thinking, "Oh, everything." So it was considered major surgery.

After any other major surgery, the recommendation is get lots of rest, don't pick up anything heavy, just relax, ask people for help, except after you have a baby and then all bets are off. Baby comes first, you come second. So trying to jump up in the middle of the night to breastfeed with a C-section scar. I mean, I remember these sharp shooting pains going down my leg, but you don't get back in bed. You have to make your way to the bassinet and you have to feed that baby.

I was so ill prepared and embarrassed by the ill preparedness, but I think it is linked to exactly what you just said, which is that culture has motherhood wrapped in a fantasy and we don't have a lot of honest language and communication and education about exactly what motherhood is going to require of you psychologically, physically, emotionally, spiritually. And it's not to scare women away from it. It's just to educate her, to help her be prepared for this choice that she's making that will be for the rest of her life.

Because unless we educate and prepare women, and maybe we're bursting a few bubbles, but it's for her own well-being in the long run. Then what we have is a woman like you and me in her fourth trimester, maybe starting during birth like me, and then in the fourth trimester we're going, "Oh my God, am I an asshole? Because I didn't think - why didn't I know it was going to be this hard? Where did I fail? How did I not prepare myself well enough? Why did nobody tell me?"

Well, we're doing this motherhood thing in a vacuum because there is not enough language around it because there's too much magic around it because it needs to be demystified. And we're doing it alone. We're not supposed to be doing this stuff alone. We're supposed to be doing it with our sisters and our aunties and our mothers and our grandmothers. This is historically - we find ourselves on this very modern island of 2026, where somehow magically after thousands of years of women doing the heavy lifting together, now all of a sudden you're supposed to have a 40 hour job and take care of the baby. I don't know how you divide yourself into two full-time jobs and then tell yourself you're only working one and the other one is a hobby.

**Aimee:** It's a sacred pastime. It's a sacred hobby. I had an early reader of the book. She told me she shared parts of the book, Good Girl, Bad Mom, with her husband. And it was very interesting because she shared with me that he said, "I think about that all the time. I think how hard women are working. I watch them come to the office and I think, man, they have balls of steel because they only slept four hours, are going to come here all day and work, then go home and probably cook dinner, do bath time."

And again, it's related to the fantasy around motherhood, the sacred hobby. Even though women and mothers, most women and mothers, innately understand and get and compute that it's not a fantasy because it's part of cultural conditioning. I think we project that fantasy on one another. I think mothers can be one another's toughest critics because even though they know the fantasy is unfair and not realistic, because we're not having this alternative conversation loud enough to say, listen, it is a joy, but it's also a job. And that's okay that you say that out loud.

We are shaming ourselves and then we're shaming other women. So we were not preparing one another for the reality. It's like we're in a room with no doors and no windows. We've created no way out for ourselves. And I think it has to start - the cultural revolution around reframing motherhood and ideally womanhood has to start with the woman herself, has to start with the mother herself, the person who is doing it.

We have to collectively align our voices and make it okay to say it is a joy, but it is a job. And I would like the job aspects of it to be acknowledged and regarded and rewarded in a much more significant way culturally than it currently is. And then hopefully - maybe we won't even be alive for it, who knows? Maybe, hopefully. Then hopefully we can stop feeling like we need to do our job as if we don't have children and raise our children as if we don't have a job.

But I don't think we get there until women, mothers first and foremost, are willing to unwrap that fantasy around motherhood and say, it is a joy, but it is also a job. It is love and it is loss. It is a miracle and it's a responsibility. So we can really give credence to both realities.

**Aimee:** I love your soapbox. Some of this is leftover - I was working on a screenplay for a long time about the feminine divine. And my partner used to say that women are the worst patriarchs. In this motherhood mystique, the concept of this magical, unrealistic expectation of motherhood as some leftover fantasy that served the patriarchy. And now that we are evolving out of the patriarchy, it is time for us as women to reclaim the truth, to stop gaslighting each other.

Let's put some neurolinguistic language around what's really going on here so that we can love each other and love ourselves enough to be honest with each other. Let's be honest about this because motherhood is awesome. I love my kids. I wouldn't trade them for anything, but was it hard to make the transition from maiden to mother in this society in this moment? Hell yeah. It was excruciatingly painful. It was really hard to do all alone, all by myself. It was really, really hard.

Is there a way that we can collectively come together and make realistic expectations? Can we really have an honest conversation about what this really is so that when it sucks, we can say, "I get that. It absolutely sucks. Some days it's so hard. Some days you get dressed to go to work and then your baby just throws up all over you. It happens."
